# TSG: Missing Metrics

Run `tsg_triage`, `tsg_config`, `tsg_workload`. Then:

1. **Check addon is enabled** ‚Äî run `tsg_config`, check "Addon Enabled in AKS Profile". If `metricsEnabled == false`, the monitoring addon isn't enabled. Customer needs `az aks update --enable-azure-monitor-metrics`
2. **Check for OOMKill / pod crashes first** ‚Äî missing metrics are often a SYMPTOM of pod OOMKills. If App Insights queries return "path does not exist", the addon is crash-looping and not sending telemetry at all. Go directly to `tsg_pods` for pod restart analysis and follow the Pod Restarts TSG below
3. **Check if the metric name exists in the account** ‚Äî run `tsg_metric_insights` with the `MDMAccountName` from triage. The "View All Metric Names" panel returns every metric name ever ingested (180-day lookback). If the missing metric is NOT in this list, it was never successfully ingested ‚Äî focus on scrape config, keep list regex, or config validation errors. If it IS in the list, the metric was ingested at some point ‚Äî the issue may be throttling, intermittent scrape failures, or dimension fragmentation (see step 3a)
3a. **Verify the metric has RECENT data in MDM** ‚Äî `tsg_metric_insights` uses a 180-day lookback, so a metric appearing there does NOT mean it is currently flowing. Run `tsg_mdm_query` with the specific metric name, cluster, and MDM account to check for recent data:
   - If `tsg_mdm_query` returns "Data exists" with active buckets ‚Üí metric IS currently flowing. The issue may be query-side (wrong AMW, PromQL syntax, Grafana time range)
   - If `tsg_mdm_query` returns "No data" ‚Üí metric is NOT currently being ingested. Continue to step 3b
   - **Always test related metrics for comparison** ‚Äî e.g. if `kube_node_labels` has no data, also check `kube_node_info`, `kube_pod_labels`, `kube_node_status_condition`. If these sibling metrics from the same scrape job DO have data, the issue is specific to that metric (not a scrape or pipeline problem)
3b. **If scrape is healthy but metric has no MDM data** ‚Äî this means the metric is being scraped but **silently dropped** somewhere downstream. Common causes:
   - **High dimension count** ‚Äî metrics with many dimensions (e.g. `kube_node_labels` with `metricLabelsAllowlist: nodes=[*]`) may be silently rejected by MDM/MStore if they exceed an undocumented dimension limit. Use `tsg_dimension_analysis` to check:
     - "Max Dimensions Across All Metrics" ‚Äî shows the highest dimension count ever stored for any metric in the account
     - "Dimension Count Weekly Trend" ‚Äî shows how dimension counts changed over time (useful for correlating when metrics stopped flowing)
     - "Current Dimension Names" ‚Äî lists all dimension names for a specific metric
     - If the missing metric has more dimensions than the observed max, it may be hitting a silent MStore rejection
   - **Keep list filtering** ‚Äî the metric may be scraped but filtered out by the minimal ingestion profile keep list regex. Check `tsg_config` ‚Üí "Default Targets KeepListRegex"
   - **ME silent drops** ‚Äî MetricsExtension may reject individual metrics without logging errors. Check `tsg_workload` ‚Üí "ME Ingestion Success Rate" and "ReplicaSet Samples Dropped"
   - **Mitigation for high dimension count**: reduce `metricLabelsAllowlist` to specific labels instead of `[*]`, or use `metric_relabel_configs` to drop unnecessary label dimensions. If the dimension limit hypothesis is confirmed, escalate to `Geneva Monitoring/MDM-Support-Core-IngestionAndStorage-Tier2` for clarification on MStore dimension limits
4. **Check AMW quota and MDM throttling**‚Äî this is the most common cause of missing metrics at scale:
   - Run `tsg_triage` ‚Üí extract `MDMAccountName` from "MDM Account ID" result
   - Run `tsg_mdm_throttling` with that `monitoringAccount` to check for throttling
   - If `ThrottledClientMetricCount > 0` ‚Üí incoming events are being rejected by Geneva. Customer is hitting their account ingestion rate limit
   - If `ThrottledTimeSeriesCount > 0` ‚Üí MStore is throttling time series. Customer has too many unique metric+label combinations
   - If `DroppedClientMetricCount > 0` ‚Üí events are being dropped before ingestion
   - If `MStoreDroppedSamplesCount > 0` ‚Üí samples are being lost in MStore
   - If event volume utilization is > 80% ‚Üí approaching limit, will start throttling soon
   - If time series utilization is > 80% ‚Üí approaching limit, need to reduce cardinality
   - **Resolution for throttling**: escalate to `Geneva Monitoring/MDM-Support-Manageability-Tier2` for quota increase, or help customer reduce cardinality via `metric_relabel_configs`
4. **Check ME ingestion success rate** ‚Äî run `tsg_workload`, check "ME Ingestion Success Rate". If `successRate < 99%`, ME is dropping significant metrics. Cross-reference with ME queue sizes and drops
5. **Check auth issues** ‚Äî look for `DCR/DCE/AMCS Configuration Errors`, `Liveness Probe Logs` with "No configuration present", `MDSD Errors`, `MetricsExtension Errors`. If `tsg_logs` shows repeated `TokenConfig.json does not exist`, this is the firewall/blocked endpoints pattern ‚Äî see TSG: Firewall / Network Connectivity. If errors mention "private link is needed", also see that TSG
6. **Check if DCR/AMW association exists** ‚Äî if `tsg_triage` ‚Üí "Azure Monitor Workspace" returns empty AND `TokenConfig.json does not exist` is in logs, the DCRA may be missing entirely. Query the `AMWInfo` data source by subscription to verify: `AzureMonitorMetricsDCRDaily | where Timestamp > ago(30d) | where ParentResourceId contains "<SUBSCRIPTION_ID>" | distinct ParentResourceId, DCRId, AzureMonitorWorkspaceResourceId`. If the cluster is absent, the addon was enabled without a proper DCR/DCRA. Use the `ARMProd` data source to check ARM deployment history and confirm when/how the addon was enabled ‚Äî see `reference.md` ‚Üí "Querying ARM Deployment Logs". **Fix:** re-enable with an explicit AMW: `az aks update --enable-azure-monitor-metrics --azure-monitor-workspace-resource-id <AMW_ID>`
   > **‚öÝÔ∏è Do NOT suggest `az aks show --query azureMonitorProfile` to find DCR info** ‚Äî the AKS `azureMonitorProfile` does NOT contain a `dataCollectionRuleResourceId`. The only way to check DCRA is: `az monitor data-collection rule association list --resource <cluster-ARM-ID>`. Also note: a DCE named "akslogs" or similar is likely for Container Insights only, not Prometheus metrics ‚Äî the customer may have a logs DCRA but no metrics DCRA.
6. **Check CPU/memory** ‚Äî if resources are very high, pods may be overwhelmed. Check if samples per minute per ReplicaSet exceed ~3.5 million
7. **Check ME queue/drops** ‚Äî run `tsg_workload`, look at `ReplicaSet Samples Dropped` and queue sizes. If growing, need HPA or more shards
8. **Default metrics missing** ‚Äî run `tsg_config`, check if default scrape config is enabled and metric is in `Default Targets KeepListRegex`. Customer may need to add metric to keep list
9. **Custom metrics missing** ‚Äî run `tsg_config` and check these queries in order:
   - **"Invalid Custom Prometheus Config"** ‚Äî if `true`, the customer's configmap has errors. Check **"Custom Config Validation Errors"** for the specific error (common: `not a valid duration string: "30"` ‚Äî missing unit suffix; `found multiple scrape configs with job name` ‚Äî duplicate job names; `unsupported features: rule_files` ‚Äî rule_files not supported in configmap)
   - **"Custom Config YAML Error Lines"** ‚Äî when the error is `yaml: unmarshal errors`, this query extracts every individual line number and field error (e.g. `line 136: field action not found in type kubernetes.plain`). This pinpoints exactly where the YAML is malformed ‚Äî typically `metric_relabel_configs` fields (`action`, `regex`, `source_labels`, `target_label`, `replacement`) placed at the wrong indentation level inside `kubernetes_sd_configs`
   - **"Custom Config OTel Loading Errors"** ‚Äî captures the full OTel collector configuration loading error chain (`Cannot load configuration: cannot unmarshal the configuration: decoding failed...`). This shows the higher-level error when the Prometheus config can't be loaded into the OTel receiver, complementing the line-level errors above
   - **"Custom Config Validation Status"** ‚Äî shows per-pod whether config was loaded (`OK`), rejected (`INVALID`), or absent (`NO_CUSTOM_CONFIG`). If DaemonSet shows `NO_CUSTOM_CONFIG` but ReplicaSet shows `OK`, that's expected (DaemonSet uses separate `-node` configmap)
   - **"ReplicaSet ConfigMap Jobs"** and **"Custom Scrape Jobs from Startup Logs"** ‚Äî verify the customer's job names appear. The startup logs query uses a wider time window since it only captures pod restarts. If empty, retry with `timeRange='30d'`
   - Check job also appears in PodMonitors or ServiceMonitors if using operator-based discovery
10. **Recording rule metrics missing** ‚Äî run `tsg_config`, check "Recording Rules Configured" to confirm rules exist. Check scrape frequency vs recording rules evaluation interval (e.g. 1m rule interval with 2m scrape interval causes gaps). Transfer to `Azure Log Search Alerts/Prometheus Alerts` if needed
11. **Target distribution imbalance** ‚Äî run `tsg_workload`, check "Target Allocator Distribution". If `targets_per_collector` is uneven or very high, some collectors may be overloaded. Check "Exporter Send Failures" for `send_failed_metric_points > 0` ‚Äî indicates ME/MDM ingestion failures
12. **Check event timeline** ‚Äî run `tsg_workload`, check "Event Timeline" to correlate config changes, restarts, and error spikes. Look for patterns like "config change ‚Üí error spike ‚Üí restart"
13. **Multi-AMW routing** ‚Äî if the cluster has multiple AMWs associated (check `tsg_triage` ‚Üí "AMW Configuration"):
    - All scrape jobs route to the **default AMW** unless explicitly configured otherwise via the `microsoft_metrics_account` label
    - **DCR-level routing vs label routing**: DCR associations route default scrape targets (kubelet, cadvisor, node-exporter, KSM) to the AMW linked in the DCR. The `microsoft_metrics_account` label on PodMonitor/ServiceMonitor relabeling routes those specific metrics to a named AMW
    - Cross-subscription ingestion IS supported without additional RBAC configuration ‚Äî the DCRA handles auth automatically
    - If customer says metrics are missing, first ask **which AMW** they are querying and **which metrics** are missing (default targets vs pod monitor custom metrics)
    - **Correct PodMonitor relabeling** (per [MS docs](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/containers/prometheus-metrics-send-to-multiple-azure-monitor-workspaces)):
      ```yaml
      relabelings:
        - action: replace
          replacement: <amw-name>  # must match an AMW associated with THIS cluster
          targetLabel: microsoft_metrics_account
      ```
    - **Common misconfiguration: wrong AMW name** ‚Äî if `microsoft_metrics_account` points to an AMW not associated with the cluster (e.g. using a prod AMW name on a dev cluster), ME will try to publish to that endpoint and **fail** with HTTP 500 / connection timeouts. Metrics are silently dropped. Diagnose with:
      ```
      tsg_query(datasource: "PrometheusAppInsights", kql: "traces | where message has 'AggregatedMetricsPublisher' and message has 'publication failed' | project timestamp, message | order by timestamp desc | take 10", cluster: "...", timeRange: "24h", outputFile: "/tmp/me-routing-errors.json", outputFormat: "json")
      ```
      The ME error pattern is: `AggregatedMetricsPublisher _Default[wrong-account.region.metrics.ingest.monitor.azure.com]` ‚Äî the bracketed endpoint reveals which AMW ME is trying to reach
    - **Cross-cluster AMW lookup** ‚Äî to find which cluster an AMW is actually associated with:
      ```
      tsg_query(datasource: "AMWInfo", kql: "search '<amw-name>' | where $table == 'AzureMonitorMetricsDCRDaily' | project ParentResourceId | distinct ParentResourceId", outputFile: "/tmp/amw-cluster.json", outputFormat: "json")
      ```
      Use `outputFile` to avoid ARM ID truncation in tabular output
14. **Pod restarts causing gaps** ‚Äî see Pod Restarts TSG above
15. **System node remediation / drain loop** ‚Äî run `tsg_pods`, check "System Node Drain Events", "System Node Drain Timeline", and "AMA-Metrics Pod Eviction Events". The AKS Node Remediator (`safeDrain` events) can drain system pool nodes for host-level health check failures (e.g. `HGefficiencypackDaemonsetReadinessLinux`, `HGCHkevlar-*`, `HGlistenerUploader*`). When system nodes are drained, ama-metrics RS pods, TA, and KSM get evicted. **Critical pattern: repeated drain loop** ‚Äî if the remediator keeps draining the same system nodes every few minutes (e.g. for recurring DaemonSet readiness failures), the ama-metrics pods get caught in a cycle: evicted ‚Üí rescheduled to another system node ‚Üí that node drained too ‚Üí evicted again. With small system pools (e.g. 3 nodes), ALL system nodes can be drained, leaving no stable location for RS pods. Symptoms:
    - Metric throughput drops ~50% and stays low for hours even though TA target counts recover
    - Metrics briefly reappear then disappear again (pods scrape once after rescheduling, then get evicted by the next drain)
    - `tsg_pods` ‚Üí "NodeNotReady by Pool" shows few/zero system nodes affected, but drain events tell the real story (drains don't always cause NodeNotReady ‚Äî they cordon and evict)
    - **Check drain concurrency:** query `safeDrain` events binned by 1-minute windows filtered to system pool nodes. If max concurrent system node drains is 1-2, then adding more system nodes (5-6+) is a viable mitigation. If the remediator drains all system nodes at once, adding nodes won't help
    - **Check who owns the remediator:** `safeDrain` reasons with `HG*` or `HGCH*` prefix (e.g. `HGCHkevlar-*`, `HGefficiencypack*`, `HGCHicm-NNNNNN-*`) are typically **customer-deployed** node health monitoring frameworks (e.g. HostGuard), NOT built-in AKS auto-repair. The customer team owns the remediation config and can adjust drain concurrency, cooldowns, or fix the underlying DaemonSet failures. Don't assume AKS platform ownership ‚Äî check the drain reason prefix
    - **Check drain reason scope:** query drain events over 7 days and check how many distinct nodes each reason affects. If a reason only hits 2-3 nodes, the issue is node-specific and adding more nodes helps. If it hits most nodes in the pool, it's a systemic issue and more nodes won't help
    - **PDB limitation:** ama-metrics has a PDB (`maxUnavailable: 1`), but it has `unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy: AlwaysAllow`. This means unhealthy pods (not Ready ‚Äî e.g. waiting for TA at startup) can be evicted even if it violates the PDB budget. When pods are evicted and rescheduled, they block at startup waiting for TA ‚Üí never become Ready ‚Üí next drain evicts them despite PDB. This makes the PDB ineffective during drain storms
    - **Distinguish from Windows-only node churn:** check "NodeNotReady by Pool" ‚Äî if NotReady events are almost entirely Windows nodes but system pool nodes are being drained, the root cause is platform-wide host remediation, and the impact on our addon is from system node drains specifically
    - **Check for concurrent mass node reimages:** if `tsg_pods` ‚Üí "NodeNotReady by Pool" shows 10+ user pool nodes going NotReady/RegisteredNode in the same window, a mass node reimage is in progress. DaemonSet pods on reimaging nodes get `NetworkNotReady` (CNI not ready) and `FailedMount` (can't sync secrets to new nodes). This creates a prolonged metric gap for node-level metrics even if RS/TA pods recover quickly. The DaemonSet disruption lasts as long as the node churn continues
    - **Check for TLS cert rotation as trigger:** if RS and TA pods failed liveness probes (503) at the same time ConfigReader logs show `inotifyoutput-ta-server-cert-secret.txt has been updated`, the trigger was TLS certificate rotation. Run `tsg_logs` ‚Üí configreader to verify. Cert rotation causes a brief (~4 minute) disruption as pods reload config and restart. If the metric gap is longer than the cert rotation window, look for other contributing factors (mass node reimages, CPU pressure on system nodes)
    - **Check cluster infrastructure pod health:** run `tsg_pods` ‚Üí "CoreDNS Pod Disruption During Incident" and "Critical Infrastructure Pod Evictions" to verify whether CoreDNS, kube-proxy, or azure-cns were also disrupted. If they were NOT disrupted but ama-metrics pods were, the issue is specific to our addon (e.g. cert rotation, liveness probe failure) rather than a cluster-wide problem
16. **Check AKS upgrade / node image change**‚Äî run `tsg_triage`, check "AKS Upgrade History" to see if the cluster was recently upgraded (version changes with timestamps). An AKS version upgrade changes the node image, which includes a new `node_exporter` version. A new node exporter can expose different/additional metrics causing TS explosion, or break scraping entirely (up=0). Signs: `tsg_scrape_health` shows `node` job degraded/down, `tsg_workload` ‚Üí "Node Exporter Sample Count Trend" shows `max_samples` changing (e.g. 2028‚Üí2065 = new version exposing 37 new metrics passing the keep-list). Also check "Scrape Samples Per Job Over Time" ‚Äî if total scrape samples are flat but TS is growing, label churn (pod/container name turnover) is the cause. If `node` job has 0% success rate after upgrade, the new node exporter version broke scraping ‚Äî escalate to AKS team
    
    **‚öÝÔ∏è Key diagnostic: `up=0` vs `up` missing ‚Äî who owns the problem?**
    - **`up=0` recorded in MDM** ‚Üí Our DaemonSet pod IS running and actively scraping, but the target endpoint returned an error or was unreachable. For node-exporter, this means **AKS broke their node-exporter endpoint** (they own it). This is NOT our addon's fault ‚Äî escalate to AKS team
    - **`up` metric completely missing** (no data points at all) ‚Üí Our DaemonSet pod itself wasn't running on that node ‚Äî no scraper to report the metric. Check `tsg_pods` for pod crashes, scheduling failures, or containerd errors. This IS our addon's problem
    - **Example:** A cluster had `node` job at ~77% success (up=0 on ~30% of scrapes) after AKS upgrade. DaemonSet pods were healthy ‚Äî AKS's node-exporter endpoint was broken. AKS fixed it and success went back to 100%
17. **Time series explosion after quota increase** ‚Äî if MDM auto-quota increased and TS count is growing exponentially, this is the "floodgate effect": the quota was throttling events, masking the true TS count. When the throttle lifts, MStore discovers all previously-dropped label combinations as new time series. Check `tsg_workload` ‚Üí "ME Throughput by Pod Type Over Time" ‚Äî if ME throughput is flat but TS is growing, the growth is from MDM catching up, not from new data. The TS count will plateau once MStore has registered all unique label combos. Watch that it stays under the TS limit
18. **Customer exporter pods unstable (not our addon)** ‚Äî if the missing metrics come from a **customer-owned exporter** (not kube-state-metrics, node-exporter, cAdvisor, or kubelet), check whether those exporter pods are healthy. **The fastest way is to check the ICM diagnostic bundle attachment** (see Step 1d):
    - Search `pods-all-wide.txt` for the exporter pod name or namespace
    - Check restart count ‚Äî high restarts (>2) indicate the exporter application is crashlooping
    - Check node placement ‚Äî if the exporter runs on system pool nodes that are being drained (age `0d` in `nodes.txt`), evictions triggered the issue
    - **Compare stability:** if the exporter pods have 4-6 restarts but other pods (including our RS pods) on the same nodes have 0-1 restarts, the exporter has an application-level bug ‚Äî NOT a node/infrastructure issue
    - **Check our RS pods too:** search `pods-all-wide.txt` for `ama-metrics-` (not `ama-metrics-node`). If RS pods also have short ages and restarts, our addon was also disrupted by the same drains ‚Äî don't claim "addon was healthy throughout" without verifying
    - Common pattern: ConfigMap `kubernetes-pods` job scrapes annotation-based targets. Use `tsg_metric_insights` ‚Üí "View All Metric Names" to identify the actual metric name, then trace back to the exporter pod. The metric name often reveals the owning team (e.g. `myteam_custom_*` ‚Üí the customer team's custom exporter)

19. **KSM scrape timeout on large clusters** ‚Äî if KSM metrics are intermittently missing or have frequent gaps, the KSM `/metrics` endpoint may be too large to serve within the default `scrape_timeout`. **Diagnosis:**
    - **KSM pod logs:** look for `"Failed to write metrics" err="write: broken pipe"` at `metrics_handler.go` ‚Äî this means our RS pod disconnected mid-scrape before KSM finished writing all metrics. The broken pipe confirms the client (otel collector) hit scrape_timeout and closed the connection
    - **Grafana verification:** run `avg by (cluster) (count_over_time(kube_poddisruptionbudget_status_current_healthy{cluster="<cluster>"}[60m]))` ‚Äî if this shows 0-25 samples/hour instead of ~60 (for 60s scrape interval), most scrapes are failing
    - **Check `metricLabelsAllowlist`:** run `tsg_config` ‚Üí "Kube-State-Metrics Labels Allow List". If the customer uses `[*]` wildcards (e.g. `pods=[*],nodes=[*]`), KSM emits ALL Kubernetes labels as metric labels, massively inflating the payload. With hundreds of nodes and many namespaces, this can make the `/metrics` response 50-200MB+
    - **Check `metricAnnotationsAllowList`:** same issue ‚Äî wildcard annotations inflate payload further
    - **KSM resource limits:** KSM default is 1 CPU core / 5Gi memory (addon-managed, customer cannot change). **However, KSM is often NOT CPU-throttled** ‚Äî typical usage is only ~12% CPU and ~7% memory. Use `tsg_workload` ‚Üí "AMA-Metrics Pod CPU (All Pods incl. KSM)" and "AMA-Metrics Pod CPU Throttling" to verify. If CPU/throttling are low, the issue is pure payload transfer time ‚Äî KSM generates the response fast but the HTTP body is too large to transmit over the network within the timeout
    - **Default scrape_timeout:** the KSM default config (`kubestateDefault.yml`) sets `scrape_interval` but NOT `scrape_timeout`, so it uses the global default of **10 seconds**. For large clusters, KSM cannot serve all metrics in 10s
    - **This is NOT a pod crash issue** ‚Äî KSM has zero restarts, zero OOM events. The pod is healthy but the HTTP response is too large for the timeout
    - **Addon pipeline will look healthy** ‚Äî TA target=1 (KSM allocated), RS processing millions of metrics, ME zero drops, queue near-zero. The problem is that failed KSM scrapes produce zero data points, so there's nothing to process
    - **MStore `Duplicated` drops are a red herring** ‚Äî check `tsg_mdm_throttling` historical baseline. If `Duplicated` drop rate is the same over 7 days, it's pre-existing and NOT related to the KSM issue
    - **Fix options (customer actions):**
      1. **Reduce `metricLabelsAllowlist`** ‚Äî replace `[*]` wildcards with only the specific labels their dashboards need. This is the most impactful fix
      2. **Increase scrape timeout** ‚Äî customer can set a longer timeout via custom scrape config in ConfigMap (override the default KSM job with `scrape_timeout: 60s`)
      3. **Both** (recommended for large clusters with 300+ nodes)
    - **KSM node placement:** use `tsg_workload` ‚Üí "KSM cAdvisor Telemetry (CPU, Memory, Node)". The DS pod co-located with KSM emits `ksmUsage` (CPU nanocores) and `MemKsmRssBytes` (memory RSS) to App Insights. The `computer` dimension reveals which node KSM runs on. This is the ONLY way to determine KSM node from our telemetry (AKSClusterMetrics recording rules strip the `node` dimension). **Note:** this is low-volume data (emitted by 1 DS pod out of hundreds), so queries may need a wider time range
    - **Prevalence:** this is NOT a rare issue. At any given time, ~20 clusters across the fleet are actively hitting KSM scrape timeout/broken pipe errors. It presents as "missing KSM metrics" and is often misdiagnosed
    - **Escalation:** if the customer needs a higher KSM CPU limit, escalate to `Container Insights/AzureManagedPrometheusAgent` ‚Äî the limit is addon-managed and cannot be changed by the customer

**Ask customer to check Prometheus UI:**
- `kubectl port-forward <ama-metrics-pod> 9090` then check `/config` (scrape config present?), `/targets` (targets up?)
- If target is down: error message has details. If `node-exporter` down ‚Üí transfer to AKS team. If `kube-state-metrics` down ‚Üí check `ama-metrics-ksm` pod logs
15. **Customer documentation:**
    - [Troubleshoot Prometheus metrics collection](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/containers/prometheus-metrics-troubleshoot)
    - [Customize scraping of Prometheus metrics](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/containers/prometheus-metrics-scrape-configuration)
    - [Default Prometheus metrics configuration](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/containers/prometheus-metrics-scrape-default)
    - [Enable monitoring for AKS clusters](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-monitor/containers/kubernetes-monitoring-enable)
